title_en: Invited to a Home Drinking Party
excerpt_en: 12/20. I spent the day advancing FOMUS’s Ireland expansion work, then went to a home drinking party hosted by a Japanese resident in Ireland who works for a major company. Surprisingly, he also ran the Medoc Marathon in September, just like me. He lives with his family, and other guests were long-term expats and locals working in Ireland. These connections are rare for students or typical working-holiday visitors, but since I’m a business owner here, the conversation fits. I learned a lot from people who are essentially elite expatriates, and it reminded me how valuable these connections are. The experiences of the last few years have been intense, and I felt my own growth again. By jumping into new environments, I’ve met people I could never have met otherwise. Those ties are priceless.
body_en:
12/20

During the day, I worked on FOMUS’s Ireland expansion,

and at night, I was invited

to a home drinking party by a Japanese resident in Ireland,

so I joined.

He works for a major company operating from Japan in Ireland,

and the interesting part is that, like me,

he ran the Medoc Marathon held this September!

Such an unexpected common point. lol

He lives with his family,

and I got to visit their home.

Other guests were also long-term expats

and people working here.

These kinds of connections are rare

for students or typical working-holiday visitors.

People living here don’t gain much

from connecting with students or working-holiday folks.

But in my case,

I’m a business owner with a working-holiday visa,

and I’m here mainly for business,

so the conversation fits,

and frankly my social experience is different.

I’m in my 20s,

but I’ve got endless stories.

People who’ve lived in Ireland a long time

sometimes find it fun

to connect with unusual people like me,

and their usual circles can get stale.

That’s why someone like me,

doing slightly different activities,

can get invited to places like this.

And for me,

connecting with long-term residents

brings a lot of learning,

and I’m meeting people in elite roles

like expats or local hires —

every one of them is interesting.

I’m truly grateful for opportunities like this.

Of course, there were people I met for the first time today,

and I shared a lot about myself,

and it reminded me how dense

my experiences over these past few years have been,

and that I’m growing day by day.

That happened because I jumped into new environments

and met interesting people,

and it reminded me again of how important it is

to keep traveling and meeting people.

I came to Ireland to expand the FOMUS brand in Europe,

and the connections I’ve made here

are people I could never have met otherwise.

These ties are truly precious,

and you can’t buy them with money.

title_en: The Direction of FOMUS I Can See After Traveling Abroad
excerpt_en: There are about 10 days left in 2024, so this is a light reflection before the year-end summary. I spent 80% of the year overseas and 20% in Japan, visiting the Philippines, the U.S., Spain, Ireland, France, Estonia, Malta, Greece, and Cyprus. I planned another Europe expedition for January–March to expand FOMUS. Traveling raised my resolution of how Japan is seen abroad and of what “masu” looks like from the world’s perspective. I’ll use the rest of the year to update FOMUS projects and my own activities, since Irish companies are on holiday. I realized my ideas about masu were becoming conventional, but the Masu Photo project succeeded in every country I visited in 2024, proving Japanese craft × photography works overseas. A recent failure was trying to sell masu in Ireland during Christmas via Instagram ads — zero sales. Still, failures gave insight, and in 2025 I’ll refresh FOMUS services and pricing, focus the brand on people who resonate with the vision, and reinvest profits into growth. Prices will rise slightly, but added value will increase. I finally put these thoughts into words in the paid version.
body_en:
There are about 10 days left in 2024.

I’ll do the big summary at year-end,

so today I want to do a light reflection,

because I’ve been able to put into words

what I’ve been thinking lately.

This year, I spent about 80% of the year overseas,

and the remaining 20% in Japan (as an address hopper).

The countries I visited were:

Philippines 🇵🇭

United States 🇺🇸

Spain 🇪🇸

Ireland 🇮🇪

France 🇫🇷

Estonia 🇪🇪

Malta 🇲🇹

Greece 🇬🇷

Cyprus 🇨🇾

Next year, from January to March,

I’ve planned another European expedition

to expand the FOMUS brand.

What I’ve seen is still just a small part of the world,

but my resolution has increased on

“how Japan is seen from overseas.”

Also, from the perspective of

“masu as seen by the world,”

I don’t think anyone has felt it more directly than me.

#Way too niche

So for the rest of this year,

I’ll use my time to update FOMUS projects

and my own activities.

The reason is that many companies in Ireland

go on holiday,

so sales outreach is difficult.

It’s better to propose from January,

which should increase the chance of being seen.

Also, after working with “masu” for three and a half years,

I feel like I’m starting to get trapped in conventional thinking.

Like, “this is what a masu is.”

I think that’s dangerous,

because proposals become too obvious.

One innovative idea I’ve kept going with is

the Masu Photo project —

taking photos with models holding masu around the world.

This project started in Japan,

but in 2024 I was able to do Masu Photo shoots

in every country I visited.

#Some countries had 10x competition

This was a big success for me,

and it showed that Japanese traditional craft × photography

as a communication tool works overseas too,

and I gathered data by country.

*By the way, this worked because I can do it now —

if I had started 10 years later, I wouldn’t have been able to.

The reason is that I’ve created a situation where

a relatively young Japanese man is traveling the world

with a traditional Japanese craft,

shows his face on social media,

and feels trustworthy.

Also, because I’m close in age to the models,

they’re more willing to take the offer.

And I always make an effort with my appearance.

The priority is to make them think,

“I want to meet this person and talk to him!”

Only after that comes “let’s take a photo holding a masu.”

So it really comes down to

“Do they want to meet me or not?”

For young models, looks matter.

Of course, when I’m in my 40s, I plan to be a cool older guy,

but doing Masu Photo from that age

would be way too high a hurdle. lol

Starting at 25

will become a big advantage in the future.

That’s a success story, but

the recent failure was

trying to sell masu in Ireland during Christmas.

It failed hard.

*We got data, so it’s not a total failure

I tried an approach where people

paid online in advance and I handed the masu to them,

using Instagram ads.

It was ignored completely.

I only paid ad costs,

and didn’t sell a single one.

In short, the approach that works in Japan —

selling an unknown Japanese craft online

and handing it over in person —

didn’t work at all in Ireland.

Of course, once I saw it wasn’t working,

I canceled the ads,

so there wasn’t a big financial loss.

I know my batting average is low,

and there’s luck and timing,

so I think every day, “What approach could work?”

Because I hit walls constantly in 2024,

I learned a lot, and in 2025,

both I and FOMUS will become stronger,

and I’ll renew each project’s services, content, and pricing.

For FOMUS masu in Japan,

I don’t need to reach everyone —

it’s enough to reach people who resonate with the vision

and want to do interesting things together.

After a few years, only those people remained,

so as a brand, FOMUS will become even more pointed (in a good way).

Right now,

mini masu are 1,000 yen,

and FOMUS masu are 2,000 yen.

That’s expensive compared to ordinary masu.

But people who buy FOMUS masu

aren’t choosing by price —

they’re buying because they resonate with the FOMUS vision,

so they’re not buying a vessel,

they’re buying a connection with me and FOMUS.

So what I should do is

raise the price a bit,

reinvest the profit into the FOMUS brand,

and spend money and time strengthening the brand.

That’s what I felt.

So from 2025,

FOMUS product and service prices

will probably rise a little.

In exchange, I’ll increase the added value.

FOMUS will keep evolving.

If that protects the people around me,

then even if someone other than customers complains

about price increases, it doesn’t matter.

I was finally able to put these thoughts into words,

so I’m outputting them here.

It’s nice that the paid version lets me write this frankly.

excerpt_en: I had plans to meet people in the evening, but the water at home barely worked, so I rushed out and worked in the city. I used the time to review how to separate FOMUS business from my personal work, confirm each business model, and set 2025 profit targets, audiences, and value propositions. Because it’s Christmas season and companies are on holiday, I decided to send proposals in January to improve success odds. The past three years were mostly preparation; from here I’ll use my experience and connections to sell masu seriously in Europe. 2024 was seed-planting, 2025 is growing those seeds, and the flowers may bloom in 2026–2027. With only about a year of working-holiday visa left, I’ll move fast. In the afternoon I had a business meeting with a Japanese contact in Dublin, then went to Jameson for a limited knit and briefly stopped by a Christmas market for hot wine. At night I met a working-holiday friend from my Philippines language school to exchange updates and perspectives on working locally versus building a business.
body_en:
I had plans to meet people in the evening,

and was going to head into the city in the afternoon,

but the water at home barely worked,

which meant I couldn’t do anything (even use the toilet),

so I got ready quickly and took the train into the city.

I worked in town during the day.

What I did was

separate FOMUS business from my personal work

and confirm each business model.

And for each business in 2025,

how much profit will I make?

Who are the targets,

and how will I provide value?

I took time to think through all of that again.

Over the next week,

it’s Christmas season and companies are on holiday,

so I judged that business emails would likely not be read,

and decided to send proposals in January.

I want to move fast,

but timing is crucial for raising the success rate.

So for now, it’s all preparation.

In 2025, my activities abroad will still be the main focus,

and I realized I need to clearly see

how much profit FOMUS should make.

Honestly, the past three years

have been the preparation phase.

Now I can do more with FOMUS,

and from here I’ll fully use my experience

and connections to sell masu in Europe for real.

I originally thought I’d start selling right away after arriving in Ireland,

but reality wasn’t that easy,

and I’ve had to learn a lot since coming here.

It’s made me realize how rarely business goes exactly as planned.

So this year’s overseas activity

was about planting seeds,

and 2025 will be about growing them.

The flowers might bloom in 2026 or 2027.

Even so, I’ll move with speed,

and I only have about one year left

to use a working-holiday visa.

You can only use a working-holiday visa

before your 31st birthday,

so I’ll move forward with no regrets.

Also, as FOMUS,

we need to generate solid profit in 2025

and create a budget we can freely use for future business.

We’ll earn foreign currency overseas

and use it in Southeast Asia and Japan —

this is a strategy that works because the yen is weak.

So far, I’ve focused on building the brand,

but from here I’ll also put even more energy

into building the budget for FOMUS!

🔻Dublin meeting

Now for the part where I met people.

In the evening, I had a business meeting

with a Japanese person I met in Dublin.

We handle different content,

but our direction is aligned,

so we discussed things we could do together.

Lately I’ve only been thinking about business,

so being with someone I can talk business with is fun.

It feels like we can move things forward.

I’ll write about it here once it’s decided.

After the meeting, we went to Jameson.

Together we went to the gift shop

to buy a Christmas-limited knit.

I normally don’t buy things,

but I bought it as a souvenir.

We also briefly went to a nearby Christmas market

and had hot wine.

All memories.

🔻Hanging out with working-holiday friends

At night, I went drinking with a working-holiday friend.

A girl I met during language school in the Philippines

chose Ireland as her second country too,

and we’ve met many times in Dublin,

but we went out for drinks for the first time in a while.

We exchanged updates.

I’m also using a working-holiday visa,

but most people spend their year

working part-time locally.

In my case, the goal is to expand my business,

so I don’t work part-time unless I really need to.

So our lifestyles are totally different.

That’s why I find it interesting,

and I can learn the differences, pros, and cons

between working in Japan and working in Ireland

from someone who’s actually doing it.

That’s a big learning for me — something I can’t experience myself.

And the reverse is true too:

the experience of having your own product

used at a Japanese Embassy event in Ireland

isn’t something anyone else is doing.

Even with the same working-holiday status,

the way we use time is completely different.

I’m not sure if this is the right way to say it, but

while most people work steadily each month,

someone like me focuses on market research,

building local connections and networks,

and always shows up when there’s a drinking meetup.

Then, when luck and timing align,

my job is to be ready to grab the chance.

Between a part-time model where income depends on hours worked,

and a business model like mine where you make revenue in bursts,

neither is inherently better — it depends on the person.

But I’m suited to building connections steadily

so I can land a big hit.

Even these drinking meetups,

going once or twice a month

lets us exchange information we can’t easily get otherwise,

so it was a very meaningful time!

title_en: Changing the Direction of FOMUS Parure
excerpt_en: With Christmas holidays starting, I stopped outreach and focused on reviewing FOMUS business models, revisions, and rebranding. I spent all day thinking about Parure. The team is three people: Massu (planning/sales), a jewelry designer, and a creator who coats the masu. After a year of small-scale testing, sales were limited and ambassador promotions produced almost zero results, even with overseas shoots. But we collected data. To hit a 2025 goal of 5 million yen profit, current sales won’t work, so we’ll shift to a higher-end strategy. Prices will rise to at least around 100,000 yen (about €600). We’ll reduce volume, target wealthy overseas markets like Dubai, Paris, and Munich, and rely on scarcity and word-of-mouth abroad rather than Japanese promotion. This is a contrarian move, but I believe it’s necessary to build a global brand. I’ll decide branding and sales strategy myself, consult the team only on execution, and push harder in 2025.
body_en:
Starting today, it’s the Christmas holiday,

and most company HQs in Ireland are likely shut down,

so I stopped outreach and emails,

and spent the day thinking deeply about

revising each FOMUS business model,

making corrections, and rebranding.

From morning to late night,

I was thinking about Parure.

The FOMUS Parure team is three people:

planning and sales (FOMUS Massu),

the jewelry designer,

and the creator who coats the masu.

We started small to see what would happen,

and over the past year we didn’t do bold promotion,

so we didn’t sell much.

We also recruited ambassadors

and tried promotions for a year,

but the effect was almost zero.

I sold masu slightly cheaper

to models around me in Japan as ambassadors,

but it didn’t lead to sales.

Even overseas, during Masu Photo shoots,

I wore Parure for photos,

but it still didn’t lead to sales.

That said,

we collected a ton of data this year.

We didn’t reach our original sales goals,

but we did sell a small amount,

so the deficit is slowly shrinking.

In 2025, I want FOMUS Parure alone

to target 5 million yen in annual profit.

If you set an annual target,

you can calculate how many units per month you need to sell,

but at the current pace, it’s extremely difficult.

So, we need changes:

price adjustments, brand growth, awareness —

multiple factors.

Therefore, in 2025,

FOMUS Parure will raise prices again.

Of course, service will become more premium,

but customers who were considering it

will likely no longer be able to buy.

Since this is the paid version, I’ll say it:

the price will be at least about 100,000 yen (around €600).

The jewelry market is interesting:

in Japan and overseas, jewelry is sold everywhere.

Cheap items start at 1,000 yen,

and high-end pieces can go over 3 million.

Looking at the future,

FOMUS needs to target high price ranges

and overseas customers,

so FOMUS Parure will shift even further upmarket.

Japanese people won’t pay 100,000 yen

just for a masu accessory.

But what if it’s a limited-edition piece

selling in Europe for 100,000 yen?

And with a certificate?

Some people would buy.

Sure, fewer people can afford it,

but we’ll also reduce volume,

and we can choose our customers,

so the required monthly sales drop,

and the product won’t flood the market.

We’ll switch to a model where it spreads slowly by word-of-mouth.

We already have data that our old approach to word-of-mouth didn’t work,

so in 2025 we’ll focus on overseas word-of-mouth, not Japan.

We likely don’t need to promote to Japanese residents in Japan.

We’ll bring it to places where wealthy people gather,

like Dubai, Paris, or Munich.

It’s a question of who we sell to and where.

Over the past year, we learned that

FOMUS Parure doesn’t resonate much in Japan

(there are people who want it, but not many),

so there’s no need to push where it won’t resonate.

This approach of raising prices and reducing customers

isn’t common in Japan,

but I believe that doing the same as everyone else

won’t make FOMUS a global brand,

so I’m choosing the opposite of the crowd.

Will results come in 2025?

Or will recognition start around 2027?

That’s unknown, but

the target destination for FOMUS Parure is now clear.

By the way,

I’m the owner, so I decide sales and branding,

and I don’t consult the Parure team on that.

Of course, there will be some changes

in execution, so I’ll confirm those.

Basically, I take guidance from

myself — who thinks about FOMUS more than anyone —

and from business owners who are currently delivering

far stronger results than me.

In 2025, I’ll push even harder

on the accessory business!!

So,

tomorrow I’ll think deeply about strategy

for the other businesses too!!!

excerpt_en: 2024/12/24–25. I spent Christmas in Ireland working while friends celebrated. Last year I did a workation in the mountains of Uenohara, Yamanashi. In Ireland, public transport stops and supermarkets close on Christmas, so there’s nothing to do outside. I took my daily walk and then stayed in, working. I updated the website for 2025, improved the Japan online store, clarified FOMUS services, and adjusted pricing: FOMUS masu stays the same, the card game SILVA goes up slightly, and Parure’s brand message will change. I raised my photography rate to 100,000 yen per shoot to reduce volume and free time for FOMUS. I spent the days editing, in meetings, and focused at my desk. Since I’d already enjoyed the Christmas market and drinks earlier, this quiet time felt like a gift. I even read a book. The shared house was decorated, too.
body_en:
2024/12/24–25

Christmas 2024 was in Ireland.

While my friends were enjoying Christmas parties

on Instagram and Facebook,

I had work-focused days.

By the way, Christmas 2023 was

a workation deep in the mountains of Uenohara, Yamanashi.

Back then I was an address hopper,

so I went to Costco with locals,

and I remember watching a movie at night

while eating sushi and cake.

In Ireland, all public transportation stops on Christmas,

and supermarkets are closed,

so there’s nothing to do outside.

I took my daily walk,

and then stayed in my room and worked nonstop.

Since I’ll be going to many countries in 2025,

I needed to update the website,

and I made detailed fixes so companies could view it with confidence.

Along with that,

I made the Japan online store easier to read.

I made FOMUS services and content more visible

and clarified each project.

FOMUS masu is the front-end product,

so the price stays the same.

The card game “SILVA” goes up a bit.

Whether I’ll sell it overseas isn’t decided yet.

If a company helps with sales, it could become interesting,

so I’ll watch a bit longer.

As for FOMUS Parure,

as I wrote in the previous article,

the brand message will be revised.

The biggest change is my personal photo services.

Until now, I charged 50,000 yen per shoot,

but with more overseas experience,

I decided to raise my value as a photographer

and increased the price.

Standard shoots are now

100,000 yen and up.

Honestly, for existing clients,

that’s probably “too expensive!”

But for me,

I want to reduce shooting time

while keeping revenue at a stable level,

and I want to invest more time and money

into FOMUS activities.

So raising my unit price

was the only conclusion.

The exact numbers differ, but

this means I now only need to shoot 50 people a year

instead of 100.

Of course, since prices doubled,

I’ll lose a higher portion of existing customers,

so I’ll need new clients too.

But if I raise my value,

the results will follow — so this is the move.

So my head was spinning

with pricing decisions

and website updates.

Except when eating,

I was glued to my laptop.

And on top of that,

there were regular FOMUS meetings,

which surprisingly became a nice change of pace.

For me, Christmas in Ireland was fine —

I already enjoyed the Christmas market earlier,

and drank with everyone the week before.

During the Christmas period,

I didn’t get invited by friends in Japan

since I’m in Ireland,

and I couldn’t invite anyone either,

so I had time alone,

which let me think deeply and read books.

For me,

that time felt like a Christmas gift. lol

By the way, I live in a share house,

so we had decorations like this too.

excerpt_en: Another full work day. Since FOMUS has separate sites for Japan and overseas, I had to build both because services and pricing differ. In Japan we offer masu sharing, community management, and consulting; overseas those aren’t active yet, while custom-design masu is the main business. Prices are also higher overseas due to taxes and the craft value. I renovated the sites accordingly. In the afternoon I prepared to release the Masu Photo book in Spain. I shot in Barcelona and Madrid on my way to Ireland, and the activity log is available for free. The print edition was already sent to sponsors, but the online release was delayed due to sponsor milestone timing and funding delays. Since funding still might come, I decided to release with their logo. I released the Spain Masu Photo book online — please check it out.
body_en:
Another full work day today!

The Japan and overseas versions of the FOMUS website

are separate sites,

so I have to build two sites.

There are two main reasons.

First, the services are different.

In Japan, we offer a masu sharing service,

community management, and consulting,

but overseas we don’t do those yet,

so there’s no point in showing them.

Instead, custom-design masu is the main overseas business,

so the placement of each project on the site differs.

Second,

the prices in Japan and overseas are different.

There are tax markups,

and since it’s Japanese traditional craft,

the prices are set a bit higher.

Even so, they still sell little by little,

so the price range isn’t wrong.

So I was doing site renewal work.

And in the afternoon,

I prepared to release the Masu Photo book in Spain.

🔻Masu Photo Book in Spain

This summer, on my way to Ireland,

I stopped in Spain.

I did Masu Photo shoots

in Barcelona and Madrid.

You can read the Spain activity log here.

*It’s free to read

America & Spain Activity Log 🇺🇸🇪🇸

The print photo books had already been distributed to sponsors,

but the online version release had been delayed.

This was due to sponsor timing.

I won’t say this publicly, but

since this is the paid version, I’ll be honest:

there’s an organization that invested in

this year’s Masu Photo shoots.

But they use a milestone system,

and the report from the last Philippines shoot

hasn’t been approved yet,

so the next Europe budget hasn’t been released.

Since they’re an overseas organization,

culture and work pace are different,

communication is hard, and things that take a day

can take a week or even a month.

The role of a leader is to gather people, things, and money —

and I’ve decided to do that by any means — but

this type of support (investment)

is new for me,

and the speed is much slower than expected.

The timing of funding and my overseas shoots

ended up badly misaligned,

so I wondered whether it was okay

to release the photo book with their logo.

In the end,

I’m still in communication with them,

and confirmed that the possibility of European funding

hasn’t disappeared,

so I released it with their logo.

Sponsors who say, “Here’s money, do what you want,”

came from my personal crowdfunding,

and that’s very easy to work with.

The organization’s investment is larger,

so they seem more cautious about the project.

Things like this are happening behind the scenes,

and I’m working every day amid that unfairness —

that’s my life overseas. lol

Anyway, that’s the backstory.

The Spain Masu Photo book is now released,

so please take a look!!

https://masuphoto.theshop.jp/p/00009

title_en: Fewer Year-End Parties This Year, lol
excerpt_en: With 2024 almost over, I finalized my business model updates based on sales and feedback, and updated the website. Since I’m living in Ireland, I can’t attend year-end parties in Japan, so I have more solo work time. I also filmed a YouTube recap of 2024 and goals for 2025, aiming to upload by Dec 30; it took about three hours to edit and insert images, so only the thumbnail remains. I also had a Parure meeting with the designer to share the updated strategy, pricing, and concept adjustments, and discussed content improvements. There’s a small chance to appear at Paris Fashion Week in March; if it happens, it will add prestige, but even without it we’ll build a strong approach. The meeting lasted two hours, and I’ll incorporate the ideas into 2025 products. With fewer parties, I drink less and feel healthier, and I’ll keep updating the site for 2025.
body_en:
There’s only a little left of 2024!

Over the year, I’ve gotten feedback on

FOMUS products and services and their sales,

and based on that,

I revised the business model for a bigger leap in 2025.

The direction is now set,

so I updated the website accordingly.

Since I’m living in Ireland now,

I can’t attend year-end parties in Japan,

so the number of parties this year is lower,

and in a way, I have more time to work alone.

I’m using that time to keep updating the website.

I also filmed a YouTube video

looking back on the year.

2024 brought huge environmental changes for me,

and I had many experiences,

so I did a light recap

and talked about 2025 goals too.

If possible, I want to upload it by December 30.

It took about three hours,

and I cut footage and inserted necessary images.

I was looking at the year’s calendar schedule,

so if I had talked about everything, it would have taken forever,

so I kept it broad.

All that’s left is the thumbnail! lol

🔻FOMUS Parure meeting

As I wrote in the previous article,

I updated the business model for the accessory brand “FOMUS Parure,”

so I shared the new direction with the Parure designer

in an online meeting.

We discussed pricing improvements and concept corrections,

and exchanged ideas on how to enrich future accessory content.

If it goes well, there’s a chance

we can appear at Paris Fashion Week in March.

If we can do that, it would add prestige to the Parure brand.

Of course, it’s not 100% confirmed,

so I see it as “lucky if it happens.”

Even without it,

I’ll build an approach that reaches customers properly.

The meeting lasted about two hours.

We had a lot of ideas,

so I’ll incorporate them into 2025 new works.

If FOMUS Parure gets on track,

it can create stable funding for FOMUS,

so in 2025,

I want to increase sales by learning from 2024.

This will require lots of testing,

so I’ll repeat the fastest possible try-and-error loop

and get stronger.

So, lately I’ve been working at home nonstop.

I take walks around the neighborhood for a change of pace

and to stay healthy. lol

*I can’t faint again

With fewer year-end parties,

I drink less often,

so this might be the healthiest I’ve been in years.

So,

I’ll keep updating the website for 2025!

excerpt_en: In the morning I worked on the FOMUS Parure website update, then had a business meeting in Ireland. After that I joined a gathering with friends in Dublin. I only knew about three people, but around eight came, and I met someone who works at a luxury hotel in Dublin — a hotel I plan to approach in January. When you keep showing up and meeting people, these chances appear. Most events don’t lead directly to work, but if you don’t move, you connect with no one. Lately I’ve realized I don’t enjoy small-talk topics like romance or gossip, and there aren’t many people in Ireland to talk business or blockchain with. I’ve accepted that I shouldn’t depend on these casual connections, and I want to focus on connecting with Irish business owners from here.
body_en:
In the morning,

I worked on the FOMUS Parure website update,

and in the afternoon,

I had a business meeting for a project in Ireland.

Then, straight from there,

I joined a gathering with friends in Dublin.

I only knew about three people,

but around eight gathered.

I got to hear a lot of stories, and

there was someone who works at a luxury hotel in Dublin.

It happens to be a hotel I plan to pitch masu to in January,

so the timing was perfect, and a connection suddenly appeared!

When you steadily show up everywhere,

these kinds of connections start to happen.

It all comes down to volume and accuracy of action.

You need some discernment,

but by going out and meeting more people,

you can create opportunities.

To be honest,

events or drinking meetups that lead to work

aren’t that common.

Still, if you don’t move,

you won’t connect with anyone,

so action is everything.

🔻Topics are hard, right?

For the past few years, I’ve only been working,

I’m interested in business,

and I have zero interest in other people’s romance or gossip.

And since there aren’t many people around me

who are running their own businesses in Ireland,

I don’t really have people to talk deeply about business

or blockchain topics.

Even when I talk to people who live normally in Ireland,

I can keep up with the topics, but

they’re not really things I want to talk about.

I have zero interest in unproductive topics

that everyone gets excited about,

and from their side, management and business talk

is unrelated to them (they’re doing part-time or salary jobs).

So I see these connections as important,

but I also feel clearly that I won’t depend on them.

The events I truly enjoy

are the ones I create myself,

and bigger isn’t necessarily better.

In Japan, I have many peers to talk business with,

but overseas, not yet.

I want to build connections with Irish business owners soon

and deepen exchange.

To put it bluntly,

my surrounding circle has solidified,

so from here, I want to think about

how to attack Irish business-owner communities locally.

excerpt_en: With 2024 ending, this is a summary post. I also uploaded a YouTube video. In short: I traveled constantly and expanded my activity worldwide, visiting 10 countries (including Japan). I published a rough month-by-month schedule: January across Japan with FOMUS activities and disaster volunteer work in Kanazawa; February–April language study in Cebu with Masu Photo shoots; May an art exhibition in Saitama and a 3rd anniversary event in Hakone; June visits to Kotohira, Takamatsu, and Sagishima; Los Angeles, Las Vegas, and New York with GPS art and couchsurfing; July Spain; August Ireland; September Medoc Marathon in France and launching international exchange communities; October Estonia; November Malta/Greece/Cyprus and a Japanese Embassy event in Ireland; December distillery visits and “masu × whiskey” research. I improved English, built a Dublin community, and progressed domestic projects. 2025 will focus on expanding FOMUS globally. Thanks for the support.
body_en:
So, as 2024 ends,

this will be the summary article.

I also uploaded a YouTube video!

If you have time, please check it out.

https://youtu.be/8rPVZ9yx1jg?si=W4jGOgOpqOCR5lt7

2024: a turbulent year

Looking back,

I traveled everywhere this year.

Even when I was address hopping all over Japan,

I moved a lot and built many connections,

but in 2024, it went global all at once.

I visited 10 countries (including Japan).

Rough schedule

January: While traveling around Japan, I continued FOMUS activities and joined disaster-relief volunteering in Kanazawa. We ran a parent–child activity using AI illustration creation. In Kyoto, I did a collaboration event with Petit Share and Nakaune Sake Shop.

February: Language study in Cebu, Philippines. Weekdays were English study; weekends were Masu Photo shoots with local models.

March: Used holidays to go to Zamboanga. No tourists, no taxis; got around by tricycle. Shot with models there too.

April: Masu Photo shoot on the beach of a 5-star hotel in Cebu. My English improved and communication got easier. After language study I went to Manila and shot there too.

May: Held the first FOMUS art exhibition in Saitama. About 100 people visited over two days. Held the FOMUS 3rd anniversary event at Hotel Senkei in Hakone, inviting supporters from across Japan. The next day I also joined a small meetup with Nishino (King Kong) and got advice on overseas business.

June: First trip to Kotohira and Takamatsu. Especially Kotohira was unexpectedly beautiful with amazing people. Also visited Sagishima in Hiroshima; started planning a new project with Sentokun. In Los Angeles I met GPS runner Shimizu-san returning from Peru; met at Dodger Stadium and missed Ohtani’s home run because we arrived late. Drew a 37 km GPS Lincoln art in midsummer. Then went to Las Vegas and New York. Activity centered on Masu Photo shoots. First-ever couchsurfing; saw both good and bad sides because hosts varied a lot.

July: Spain. Masu Photo shoots in Barcelona and Madrid. The inside of Sagrada Familia was incredible. Mostly focused on exchange. Couchsurfing at a nudist’s home.

August: Arrived in Ireland, the main destination. Started activities in Dublin. Attended language school in the mornings only, with homestay during that period. Struggled to understand Irish English. Joined many events to build local connections.

September: Medoc Marathon in France. Pre-party, 42 km marathon, and after-party — a dream three days for wine lovers. Made FOMUS uniforms. Might join again next year. Established an international exchange community in Ireland. Held weekly walking events and also events for the original card game “SILVA.” Now the community totals over 1,000 people.

October: Estonia. Masu Photo shoots in Tallinn, lots of drinks with locals. Joined a Japanese exchange event in Estonia. It happened to be a cooking event where we made Estonian food.

November: Masu Photo shoots and exchanges in Malta, Greece, and Cyprus. Provided masu and sake at an event at the Japanese Embassy in Ireland.

December: Visited multiple whiskey distilleries and explored the potential of “masu × whiskey.”

There’s too much to write,

but 2024 was a year of earning with my own feet.

I visited 10 countries and did Masu Photo shoots.

Being able to communicate in English (a bit)

expanded my activities and allowed deeper conversations.

In Ireland, I launched an international exchange community

to spread FOMUS and created spaces with 10–20 people each time.

More people became interested in masu.

This year, investing in an English environment

made communication smoother.

*Business English still needs more training.

I was also moving domestic projects behind the scenes,

and plan to announce them in early January.

Because I traveled both Japan and overseas,

I gained many insights,

and I deeply felt that both FOMUS and I

need to earn foreign currency properly.

In 2025, I’ll use everything I’ve learned and all connections

to expand the FOMUS brand globally.

Thank you for your continued support!

excerpt_en: It was New Year’s Eve. I had already finished my 2024 recap and Instagram content, so I used the time to write out my 2025 goals. Clarifying goals makes them more achievable. I wrapped up FOMUS work during the day, then went to a home party with Irish friends at night. I drank a lot for the first time in a while and welcomed 2025 happily. Many young people went to countdown fireworks, but we preferred a cozy party. I got home when the trains started running in the morning — tired and tipsy, but safe. Days like this shouldn’t be too frequent, but a few times a year are necessary. I didn’t repeat the full recap, but this year taught me how to navigate a new environment, deliver a brand, and keep trying. The experience itself is invaluable. In 2025 I’ll grow FOMUS’s IP further and build a profit system. Thank you for your support.
body_en:
It’s New Year’s Eve.

By yesterday, I had already finished

the 2024 recap and creating Instagram posts and videos,

so at this timing I wrote out various 2025 goals.

I believe that clarifying goals

makes them more achievable.

I worked on that during the day,

and wrapped up FOMUS work for the year.

At night, I went to a home party with Irish friends.

For this time, I stopped thinking about work,

and drank a lot for the first time in a while.

I got pleasantly tipsy

and welcomed 2025 with everyone.

Since I’ve made many friends since coming to Ireland,

I can see what everyone is doing on Instagram,

and the younger people went to watch countdown fireworks.

We’re older now,

and we don’t like the cold or crowds,

so having a party with friends was just right.

In the end, I got home

when the trains started running in the morning.

I was drunk and sleepy,

and even missed my stop on the train,

but I got home safely. lol

If days like this happen too often,

it’s tough for health and work,

but I think a few times a year are necessary. lol

*It’s just fun.

🔻2024 ends

In the previous article, I already wrote the recap and YouTube,

so since it’s New Year’s Eve,

I didn’t write the activity details again, but

my environment changed drastically, and within that,

I kept thinking about how to navigate,

how to deliver the brand,

and I kept working through trial and error.

This experience itself

is an asset you can’t buy with money,

and I believe I became stronger this year.

The role of a leader is to gather people, things, and money,

and while it’s hard to see from the outside,

this part was truly tough,

but I did reach my target line.

In 2025,

I’ll further grow the FOMUS brand IP,

operate it,

and build a system that can generate solid profit.

Please continue to support me in 2025!
